speaker: interviewee
text: The life in the army was very-- it was very hard. It was very strong. I never liked the army up till the end, from the beginning, and up to the end. But at that time, it was very, very difficult to be there in the army. And the discipline was very high. They used to, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] take an exercise in the army, like-- like in any army. But before that, they used to take like eight hours. But prior to the war, and probably there was any feeling that the war will come, they used to take exercise for 14-- for 16 hours. So it was very hard. But I was lucky at that time because of my profession as a technician. So I was building some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some buildings for the army there. So I was lucky, until the war begins. But the-- the harsh time was after the war begins. Like I said, we fled back, and we stopped at a city in Ukrainia-- in the Ukraine, Malyn. And we take a defense. This is a-- the remainders of our regiment. And it-- it remained less than a half. Otherwise, some of them were killed, some of them were prisoners, were imprisoned there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there for a month-- I think for a month and a half. And then rumors, you know. We didn't know what's going on at the front. And then rumors began-- beginning to spread that the Germans, the German army, is far ahead of us. And we are, you know, at the rear of the army. And this-- and that was really-- it was bad. So from the sides, you know, the German army, from both sides, they go-- they are going around and around. And they are circled the armies. And they took them, like I said before, it's-- the Germans called it castle. It's a pot. It's a boiling pot. So they-- they took us in this boiling pot. And then they started to split, you know, parts and parts, and killed and imprisoned them. But there was 600,000 soldiers and officers with the ammunition, with everything. So they-- it was a very big defeat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we started, you know, I joined then some soldiers with a truck. I joined them, and we tried to escape from this-- it was a corridor left, and we tried to escape from this corridor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in July 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- where did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Ukraine. At the Ukraine. So we started in Malyn, like I said. And we started to-- to escape to cross the Dnieper River, because the Dnieper River was really an obstacle. And all the bridges, they were taken either by the Germans, or they were exploded by the retreated Russian army. OK. And then we were lucky. We-- we crossed the river, and I was thinking, now we are-- we are free. We can join the-- the main Russian army. But it was wrong. We have been in this, the castle, this. The circle was wider and wider, and we are still in the circle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there we went to-- to another city, Chernigov. And that was the same thing. The Germans, from there, they were bombed us day and night, day and night. And it was-- everybody was moving. And we were moving only at the nighttime, because it was very dangerous to move at the daytime.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the roads, they were full of soldiers, vehicles, civilian populations, even cattle-- horses, and cattles, and pigs. Everywhere-- everything was there. It seems like the whole world was moving there. And the many Jews trying to escape, they have been there, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stopped at a small city. Remember, what was it called? I think [INAUDIBLE], was it called, near Chernigov. Near Chernobyl. You heard about Chernobyl. It's near Chernobyl. We stopped there, and that was the final. I felt this is-- this is the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a friend of mine and I, we used to, you know, we digged a hole, and we used to stay in this hole. And so we stayed there. It's a trench. We stayed in the trench for two days. And then we heard the-- the Germans are here. We heard shooting. The Germans are already here.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he wasn't Jewish. He was Russian. But he was a good guy. And it was almost night. He said to me, you know what? Let's-- let's go in different directions. I don't want-- I don't want to betrayed you. I said, OK. We hugged each other, and I went away. So I went to another village all alone. And I tried to be alone. To-- I don't want to see, you know, friends who knows me, because I was the only Jews there. So I was stuck in a village. And there have been many soldiers and officers. And everyone is-- everyone is talking what to do, what we are going to do. So I found-- it's-- I found a home full of soldiers. And, you know, I was young and tired. And I go to sleep. But, on dawn, I heard very noisy tanks and shootings. I went out. And then I heard the Germans. I can saw them. The Germans are here. So for me, to be captured, you know, to be a prisoner of war, that's-- that's impossible. And there was-- I see there was a-- a river. So I went to the bank, down. And there was a small boat. I picked up the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another soldier was here. Like I told you, he was Armenian. He said, let's go together. I said, go together. And we-- we are trying to cross the river. But we couldn't cross the river, because the river was muddy, and a lot of bushes and thicket was in the river, and the reed, so we couldn't see the opposite side. And it-- it took us all the day until I saw it's night. We were exhausted there-- and cold and wet. And that was September-- September 20th.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we find-- suddenly we see an isle, a small isle. And there was a stack of a hay. So we ran into the hay wet and cold. So we hugged each other, and we fell asleep there. Until the morning, we woke up. I can see the sun is high. We got to go from there. So, again, into the water. And it took us a few minutes. And we have been at the-- at the other bank of the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly I-- I saw-- all of a sudden I saw a peasant there. He was working in his field. So he-- he came up to us, and he said, there's no Red Army here anymore. There's no Germans, either. The Germans, they are in this city and this city, but not in this village. And he saw us. He looked at us, how we are wet and exhausted. He said, let's go to my home. And he gave us some borscht to eat, and some bread. And then he took our clothes-- good clothes. And he gave-- [CHUCKLES] gave me an old clothes. He said, he was laughing, he said, this is my grandfather's clothes. Old shoes. Everything old. And he gave us a piece of bread, salt, and matches. And the other guy, he didn't know. He thought I'm-- I'm Jewish. But I go. And I was thinking, now I'm going to the Hell. That was the beginning of my journey. Really the beginning of my journey to the Hell from now on. From September the 20th, 1941. That was-- everything what was before is nothing in comparisons with-- with this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get into that, I just want to ask you, were there any other Jews in your unit in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- yes. It was one Jew. He was a friend of mine. He was an officer, a commissar. And he was killed before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His last name was Sarafkin. His last name was Sarafkin. But I don't remember his first name. He was a good guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other soldiers know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone knows. I'll tell you later on why that that was the problem why they changed my first name, you know? They used to-- to call me that. They used to tease me.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: During this time. Even before the war, in the army. Even before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is it. They used to-- to tease me. Actually, it was-- it was an order in the army. You got to call everyone by his name, by his last name. And some of them, even officers, they didn't call me by the last name. They called me by the first name. Yeah, Avrom, Avrom. You know. So they-- they used to tease me. And that's why, after that-- that all, I decided to-- to change my first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you changed it to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Mikail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated in general? I mean, being a Jew with all these non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad, because I was in a position. Some-- even some officers depended on me. Like I said, I was building, you know, some homes. And they need-- they need apartments. And it was somehow depended on me. So they-- they were good. Everyone was-- was good. And I was very close, you know, to the commanders, and they knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the commanders? Do you remember any names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, yeah. The commander of the regiment was Kutarev. His name was Kutarev. And the chief of staff, the chief of staff was-- I don't remember his name. But I knew he-- I knew he was killed. And the-- and the head of the-- of the regiment, he was killed, too. At the very first days, you know. It was a tragedy. People you-- you knew. You just saw him. Now, they are no more. And that's all I think. No more Jews was, you know. Our regiment, it was more than a thousand people, and no more like us. The two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that man who-- who you were friends with who said we should part company, do you remember his name? Who didn't want to have to turn you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: With who I walked?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. And in-- in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Armenian man?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. The man before that. You said [INAUDIBLE] your army friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the-- the Jewish man?

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't think he was Jewish, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the friend-- I saw your, his picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is this one? You mean this one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is later on, not--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. I know. But you said that when you had-- when you were-- when you were going to hide, you left with another man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh. Yes. Just a minute. What's his name? Koslow was his name. Yeah, he was-- he was-- as a matter of fact, he was very bright, and-- and a good man. Intelligent. Yes. Koslow. He was at Belarus. But after that, I never met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the village that you hid in, was there a name to that village? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it's 1941, and now your troubles really begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now my troubles really begin. First, fear. Only-- only fear and only fear. You know. And-- and that was my life for a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear and desperation. Fear because, you know, you can be caught any day, any hour, any moment-- and killed. But not only killed. You could be tortured and then killed. Later on, I was not afraid to die, you know. But how to die. That was it. And desperate, you know. Because I haven't seen-- I had nothing. It seems to me at that time, probably not only to me, that the Germans, they will seize the-- the world. It was so easy for them to seize city by city, city by city. There's no-- there's no resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened between-- after you-- you had left the army and now you were in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you-- want to tell the story. It wouldn't be enough this time. But I want to tell you some harsh moments.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after we went with this guy, we didn't go to any village. Three weeks in a row, we used to sleep at the field and to eat potatoes, beets, corn, and some apples. I found a helmet. I had-- like I said-- I had matches, and that was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was cold. I got a cold. I couldn't sleep anymore, you know, outside, like a dog. And then we went to one village, to the other village, to villages where there was-- where no Germans. And we started to asking for some work. And they give us some food and a-- a spot to sleep. And in the morning, or in the other morning, we went further.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came up-- and that was October already. Both of us, we came up to one village, and there was again a river. Like I said, the rivers were the-- the main obstacles. How to cross the river? No bridges. There is a bridge. It's in the city of Poltava. You can't go. I have no identification. They will capture me at-- at the same moment. So I ask a peasant, how can-- we can swim very good. Can we cross the river? He said, yeah. The river over here is very narrow. It's five kilometers. Go in. There is a forest, and you'll do it quietly.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came up, you know, to the edge of the forest. And I can see ten more people on-- nine-- nine more people there-- nine men and woman, a nurse. They are people like us. And they are thinking the-- the same, how to cross the river. We join them, and talking. But suddenly we heard, Halt! Halt! Partisans! And I can see about 15 or 20 German soldiers here. So we spread, all of us, at the same second. I fell down near a bush. In a few minutes, they captured all of them except of me. I didn't believe myself. All were captured. And one of them, he goes around. And I can feel-- I bend myself. I can feel he'll step on my head. But that was OK. He went by. And, you know, I was so sweaty and fell asleep there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on in the evening, a woman, a peasant, it was her garden there. She came up and woke me up. I was [? afraid. ?] And she said, how did you survive? It couldn't be. She said, you know what they did? They killed all of them. All the people they killed. You want to see it? They are-- they are lying there. And she said, you want to go to my home? I went there. And I was shaking like that. And she gave me warm milk and-- and a place to sleep. It was cold already. And the Ukrainians, they have ovens, you know. You can sleep over the oven, over there. So I-- I fell asleep there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning-- when I woke up in the morning, it was rashes. All over here I got rashes. So but I got to go. She gave me a piece of bread. And I went again to cross the river. It was flurries already. It was October 20, or 25, like this. I took off my clothes. Despite the-- the snow, I swim. I cross the river swimming. And then I put on my clothes very fast, and I ran. I ran about five kilometers just to warm up. And that was one. And I'm still alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, again, a very risky-- a very risky moment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, OK. It was winter already. And now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am in-- near Khar'kov. Yeah, near, near Khar'kov, near Chuhuiv, and near the city of Khar'kov. Khar'kov is big city. Near the- near the city-- no. Near the city of Kupiansk, it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I settled. I stopped at a home. And I said-- oh, I stopped at a home. Is there anything I can do for you? Then she said, there's nothing to do, but come in. She saw me who I am. Come in. Sure, she gave me something to eat. But there was another guy there. He was a cobbler, a shoe repairman. But he is the same guy like me. He is just sitting there, you know, to-- to warm up, too. And he said, do you know how to repair shoes? I said, no, I have no idea. OK. Sit down. I'll teach you. And he-- we worked-- so we did something together. We stayed there for two days, I think. On the third day, at the village, full of Germans. Full of German soldiers. The front was only three kilometers near us. But my goal-- what was my goal? My goal was to join again the Red Army. That's why I was walking and-- and looking for. Otherwise, I did not imagine a life, you know, other life. What kind of life was it for me? To be a beggar? What kind of life is it? And to-- to be catched and to be killed anytime? So in a few minutes, the-- an older man come in and said, guys, you got to go with-- you've got to follow me to the Commandant, because this is the front. So you can't stay here. Well, what can I say? Nothing. OK. Stay here. I go to the next-- to the neighbor. There are, it seems to me, there are some soldiers there, too. I'll-- And I'll take-- I'll take you, all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same very moment, you know, I reacted, very sharply. I said to the guy, let's go. Let's go. He said, no, I'm not going. I am going-- I am going to-- better to be a prisoner of war. I'm exhausted. I'm not going. Well, he was a Russian guy, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. I won't stay. At the very moment, I started to fled. But the woman, she said to me, you know what? Don't go anywhere. If you'll go through the village, you'll be captured in a few minutes. Go through the back yards, and at the very end of the street lives my mother. And you tell her I said to let-- to let you go in and to-- to sleep there. And that it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at the-- and the next morning, I was really exhausted. And I-- I can't stand anymore here. I got to go back to the rear, you know, to-- to the west. And went back for, maybe, 30 kilometers. Again, I find a home as a-- as a shoe repairman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked there. I did everything there. But this-- this village was 80 miles-- or 80 kilometers from the city of Khar'kov. And at the same time, in Khar'kov used to live over hundred thousand Jews. And at the same times, they started to deportate all Jews from the city-- and, not far away, to a village. And they killed all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the villagers, the young villagers, every day, they went by sleds to Khar'kov to loot the-- the Jewish homes. And they bring goods every day. They used to bring goods in at there-- the home where I was sitting-- they gathered all together, 10, 15, 20 people, and they're bragging each other. I got this. I've got this. And I'm sitting and doing something, but my heart was bleeding. And it was one guy, an old guy. I remember his name-- [NON-ENGLISH]. A [INAUDIBLE] Ukrainian guy, [NON-ENGLISH], with a big cross here, a wooden cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started, you know, to talk to me. And I didn't like this. Asking me, where are you from? What are you doing? What were you doing? Where are your parents? What is the name of your parents? I didn't like it. And, on the fourth day, maybe on the fifth day, the-- the owner of the home, it was evening, he said to me, [? Ivan, ?] you know what? This guy [NON-ENGLISH] said you're Jewish. I said, no, I'm not Jewish. Well, he started to yell at me. What do you mean? You know what? I could be killed by the Germans. Do you have any identification cards? And I said-- I was lying-- I said, yes, I do, but I left them at the Commandant. OK. Go and bring it to me. Otherwise, I'll take you to the Commandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the same second, I fled his home. It was so cold. It was so cold. It was-- the frost was 30-- 35 Celsius degree. It was so windy. And I was almost naked-- you know, in rags. I was dressed in rags. Even the shoes was so ripped, I should wrap them in rags to-- to go. But I got to go. I got to fled.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 8 o'clock in the evening. I don't know where to go. And why-- why do I have to go? I don't know it. So I went out. And again through the back yards, I ran. It was so cold. I can feel it, you know, inside. Inside I can feel it. My stomach is cold. But I ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the midnight, I-- I didn't know where I go, what I do. I didn't know. But I remember it was so cold. And the moonshine. So I ran and ran. And suddenly I saw-- do I have to stop [INAUDIBLE].
